trainees.name: Michael Wilson
trainees.current_summary: July 7 was a selling-themed night he sailed through as usual. Asked what he does best, he called himself a 'jack of all trades, master of none.' He sold his bump and his line well, then took the coaching role on spot one — teaching Damian through it (Damian's first time) with a good full-speed run. In the four-corner drill he worked Hailey through the corners and then sold well himself, and cut a solid one-minute promo. No flags on his own work. June 30 was a quiet, clean night. Joey arrived late and only caught Michael's warm-up (no flags). He helped demonstrate chain wrestling for the group alongside Cade, then worked a five-minute practice match as the heel against Brandon — a strong control segment (wrist-lock finger work, forearms, chops, choke spots, repeated near-falls) before eating Brandon's comeback and losing to a Vader bomb. No flags on his own work. June 23 was a low-key night where he again did clean fundamentals (no notes on dropdowns with Brent, hip toss, or arm drag) and stepped up as a helper — boosting guest trainer Kytel for an assisted moonsault attempt. He then volunteered to TAKE a time warp for the first time, getting pre-coaching on form from Cody and Kytel; Jimmy moved the session along before his attempt, so his execution wasn't logged. In his five-minute practice match he worked a polished heel performance against Brandon: big boot, European uppercuts, the claw, shoulder tackle, dropkick, reverse foot lariat, repeated near-falls, and a clean finish (hip toss → kick → pin Brandon didn't kick out of). No flags on his own work. June 16 was another clean senior night: lockup flawless on the second rep (both bumped on the first), sped-up strikes that the trainers specifically liked, a good DDT take and a good DDT give (paired with Jeff), and a perfect enzuigiri on both attempts. No corrections on his own work. June 12 was another clean, leadership-heavy night: zero notes on his own work (clean front rolls, good superman rolls, no notes giving headlock takeovers to the whole group), and Cody had him lead the gym in teaching the fireman's carry — he demonstrated and gave every trainee one. He also took a kitchen sink (new move) well and worked his own drop-kick form between other trainees' reps. Continues as the de facto senior coach. June 9 was another clean night: very good big boots, no notes on his punches, helped Dalton work blocking (taking forearms in response to Dalton's punches), worked the tag finisher with Hailey, and practiced sentons on the punching bag. Senior trainee acting as a de facto coach, and the gym's model for self-driven ringwork. June 2 was a short night cut off when the ring broke: he did well on back rolls, then he and Brandon were trying a new leg-drop move when the ring gave out and training paused for promos; earlier he was sent to work lockups with the new trainees Jacob and Damian. May 26 was a clean bumping session — good snap bumps with no notes, no feedback needed on either shoulder-tackle or back-elbow rounds, and in Cade's heat drill he was made the explicit example for the whole group: always getting back up and moving on his own without relying on Cade to prompt him, with Cade telling the others they should all be doing the same. May 19 fit the pattern: Jimmy used him (with guest trainer Cade Powers) to demonstrate correct suplex form for the group, and he gave Dalton a body slam with no notes on his execution. He also wrestled the night's standout practice match against Tyler — chained lockup/wrist-control reversals into a short chain-wrestling spot, a believable failed-shoulder-tackle sequence built around his size, selling a suplex and a knee before firing back with punches, and eating the finishing body slam. Trainers called it more technical refinement than correction; Jimmy's only note to the pair was to re-attempt shoulder tackles immediately off the ropes rather than circling. On May 15 served as the drop-toe-hold giver for the whole face-bump block and coached Jack through his first take. Continues to draw zero flags on his own work.

trainees.name: Hailey Stokely
trainees.current_summary: July 7 (selling night): asked what she does best, she said forearms and selling. She sold her bump and line well and continued in her usual near-trainer role — paired with Jack on spot one and helped refine his hip toss form, and in the four-corner drill she worked Jack through the corners and sold well herself when Michael worked her. Her individual promo wasn't logged this session. No flags. June 30 fit the pattern. Joey caught her warm-up (no flags). She chose chain wrestling as the night's focus and led teaching it to the newer trainees — Jimmy backed her point about always watching your opponent — and worked chain wrestling with Kytel herself with no notes. In her five-minute match she beat Dalton with a DDT while calling and leading the match, then afterward flagged Dalton for skipping a move she'd called. Veteran trainee operating at near-trainer level. June 16 was another effortless night: clean on the lockup with new trainee Jaden (she asked to run it twice, both good), very good selling, and on strikes she asked Cody for coaching on alternative methods rather than criticism-based refinement and did well. She walked through the DDT process before taking it cleanly, gave a clean DDT (paired with Jaden), and insisted on doing drop kicks without the crash pad — nailing both attempts. Veteran trainee setting the standard. June 9 again showed her coaching eye and polish: she flagged the off timing on Damian and Jacob Renzo's double down before Jimmy worked it, added a match-application note about discreetly checking on your opponent after big spots, and flagged Dalton twice — once on his kick form (knee pause) and once on his dangerous closed-fist punch (noting it would have seriously hurt Brandon if it connected). She also worked the tag finisher with Michael and did a super-kick form refresh with Jimmy and Cade, kicking much better after. Veteran trainee operating at near-trainer level. June 2 fit the pattern: great back rolls, an impromptu rope-running and dropdown drill with guest trainer Kytel, helped break in the new trainees by pairing with Jacob Renzo on lockups, and capped the night cutting a great promo with Jimmy. May 26 was effortless: clean snap bumps with no feedback needed across both the clothesline and back-elbow rounds, and on shoulder tackles she did well and even requested an extra attempt to refine the technique on her own initiative. Sailed through the heat drill (did very well). The kind of session where she draws zero correction and quietly sets the standard. May 19 underscored it: took the suplex flawlessly and the body slam without issue, then Jimmy used her twice as the demonstration model — first for stiff 'board' body-slam form for the group, then to show that size doesn't matter when giving a body slam (she gave guest trainer Kytel a clean body slam after some form coaching). Capped the night with a polished heel performance in a five-minute match against Nick: stalled the start ('match starts when I'm ready'), worked the hand-switch fake and a foot stomp, ran wrist-lock-to-headlock and a shoulder tackle, then sold a hope-spot loss — Nick caught her with drop-downs, three clotheslines, and a roll-up for the finish. Not at May 15. May 12 she effectively co-ran the spot-one block — coached Jack through every unfamiliar piece (lockup-to-headlock transition, leapfrog form including where to bend and how low, hip toss form including foot placement and jump mechanics), then drilled the jumper role on the leapfrog with him (her first time as jumper) until she felt comfortable. Hit spot one flawlessly at full speed with Jack, then volunteered to run it a second time with Jeff to balance the odd numbers — also clean. Ran the second-rope hurricanrana on Jimmy on the turnbuckle and worked satisfaction with Michael. One self-inflicted clip on her first frog splash off the second rope — hand placement was slightly off. Caught DC's head dropping during the headlock, and shut down his promo cleanly with Jimmy when he crossed a personal-insult line. Currently locking in the hurricanrana and a satisfaction ahead of the next TAW show.

trainees.name: Jacob Justice
trainees.notes: Marked inactive May 12, 2026 after six consecutive absences from April 7 onward. Not expected to return to training.
trainees.current_summary: Selling visibly improved through early spring, but he hasn't been at a session since April 7 — six consecutive absences and counting. Confidence and pacing were the biggest gaps when he was active: flagged as timid and slow on rope-throw drills on April 7, and flagged as an injury risk for landing elbow-first on bumps. Hard to track progress with this much absence.

trainees.name: Tyler Skelton
trainees.current_summary: June 30: worked chain wrestling paired with Jeff and did great (Jeff was the one reminded on headlock head placement); Jimmy coached the pair on body placement and transition work. Tyler sat out the practice matches after hurting his knee at work. June 12 was a mixed but solid night. Clean on front rolls; his first couple superman rolls were a little rough but improved after. No notes giving headlock takeovers when it was his turn. His one struggle was on drop kicks — like Jeff, he had difficulty getting enough jump height/altitude to land them cleanly, a size/explosiveness edge to keep working. Big-frame trainee still learning how his height and size shape his ringwork. May 19 was a strong outing: brief hesitation on the suplex then took it cleanly, and stepped in to learn how to GIVE a body slam, executing it just fine. He also worked the night's standout five-minute practice match against Michael — chain-wrestling reversals, a failed-shoulder-tackle sequence playing off Michael's size, a corner splash, a suplex, and a multi-step setup into the finishing body slam. Trainers framed their feedback as refinement (Jimmy: re-attempt shoulder tackles off the ropes; Cody: volume, selling, pacing) rather than correction. May 15 had a clean lockup with Jeff (no notes) and a believable one with Michael, plus a clean run as the drop-toe-hold giver with Brandon after some execution pointers from Jimmy on the second attempt. The flag from this session was on the face-bump receiving end — he was using his hands too much to catch himself during the drop-toe-hold face bump, and Joey read it as mostly psychological. Jimmy's face-bump spot-check after the initial drills was clean for him. May 8: handled front rolls cleanly after Cody form coaching, took the kick + back forearm with no notes. German suplex came together cleanly on April 24. Not at May 12.

trainees.name: Jay Taylor
trainees.notes: Removed from program April 14, 2026. Blackballed from local wrestling scene due to unsafe work, lack of attendance, refusal to accept criticism.

trainees.name: DC
trainees.notes: Removed from the training program May 15, 2026 by unanimous Wrestler's Court vote following the May 12 promo incident (personal insults toward Hailey). Jimmy, Cody, and Kytel determined Wrestler's Court was the most impartial way to handle the decision. DC pled his case in front of the trainees; each trainee voted in the locker room with reasoning. DC's defense involved disturbing allegations — locker-room vote notes kept separately at Joey's call, not in this record. Prior conduct flag: May 12 promo, called Hailey a bitch, crossed other personal lines.
trainees.current_summary: Removed from the program by unanimous Wrestler's Court vote on May 15, 2026. He sat out the front of the May 15 session in a meeting with Jimmy, Cody, and Kytel about the May 12 promo incident, then pled his case to the trainees before the vote. Technical record before removal: form fundamentals still needed polish at the end — stiff lockups, head dropping during the headlock, drifting on the two-person rope-crossing drill, timing/coordination off on his first spot-one attempt on May 12. The decisive issue was conduct, not technique: in promos opposite Hailey on May 12 he tried to make her go first 'because she's a woman' despite her being the heel, then in his own promo got too heated and called her a bitch among other personal lines. Jimmy and Hailey stopped the promo on the spot and made clear that personal insults were a hard line; Wrestler's Court three days later closed the program out.

trainees.name: Dalton
trainees.current_summary: July 7 was again the most coaching-intensive night of anyone, this time on spot one. He sold his bump and line well early. But spot one was rough: his first attempt (with Michael) was cut short because he didn't remember the placements, and his run with Jeff drew a stack of flags — incorrect finger-lock form in the headlock, not switching his feet, and not remembering who takes the shoulder tackle. Jimmy walked him through it step by step; on the restarts he went too fast and lost the thread, then on a from-the-top restart was flagged again for incorrect headlock hand placement, moving too slow on the shoulder tackle, and running crooked. His final restart was slow but passable. In the four-corner drill he was flagged for doing the same moves all around the ring when working Damian (no notes on the selling/receiving end). His promo had a strong start but fizzled out after about 45 seconds. Still the trainee absorbing the most correction. June 30 stayed on the coaching-heavy pattern. On chain wrestling with Kirkland he was reminded to keep his head up during a headlock, but otherwise did a good job. In his five-minute match he lost to Hailey (who was leading and calling the match) and drew two flags: Hailey flagged him for not doing a move she'd called, and Cody flagged him for not selling enough — though overall it was deemed a passable practice match. June 23 stayed true to the pattern. On dropdowns (paired with Brandon) Brent flagged him for moving too slowly. On hip tosses he was told to get more height and to sell, given a couple more reps, reminded by Jimmy to stay straight and not come in crooked — the fourth was better and the fifth and final was good. On arm drags his first attempt was clean, his second he landed wrong (no one could figure out how), and his third was good again. He also drew two in-match flags in his five-minute practice match against Brent (a heel-control match where he ate most of the offense before short comebacks): no-selling (Jimmy) and not moving enough (Kytel). Still the trainee getting the most correction, but absorbing it within the session. June 16 was once again the most coaching-intensive night of any trainee. Lockup: stopped for coming in too stiff (coached by Jimmy and Cody), then flagged for not bumping in the middle and for walking into the final lockup rather than working into it — the re-do was his best attempt yet. Strikes: came in too slow and soft, then over-corrected to too fast, so Cody demonstrated winding up for one good strike and letting it breathe; Jimmy then gave him an alternative punch he executed better. DDT giving: put Damian in a headlock on the wrong side, and Jimmy advised landing more flat-backed, less butt. He improved across his drop-kick attempts (first lacked rotation), and his enzuigiri genuinely came together by the end — the final couple were really good and the trainers were proud of the execution. Still needs a lot of correction but keeps absorbing it within the session, and is increasingly stringing together clean work. June 12 was again the most coaching-intensive night of any trainee, this time on the headlock takeover drill. Front rolls were clean and his superman rolls were adequate (form could've been better). On headlock takeovers as the giver he drew correction after correction: Cody flagged him turning too hard on the drop and jerking the opponent around; Jimmy flagged him walking during the foot switch before the drop; he landed on Jack's head on one rep; and he was flagged again later for foot directionality. Cody gave him a headlock takeover so he could feel the move from the receiving end, but his final attempt (with Jeff) was still not great — headlock takeovers are clearly his new growth edge. He did, however, do well on drop kicks after a first-attempt correction. Absorbs correction but needs a lot of it. June 9 was a heavy-correction night with two safety flags. On double downs his forearm exchange needed work; after blocking work with Michael and one-on-one from Jimmy on the post-up forearm exchange, his forearms (giving and taking) looked much better. On big boots Hailey flagged his kick form (raising knee, pausing, then continuing) — fixed after a fluidity correction. The two concerning moments were both safety: on punches he threw way too hard with a closed fist (Hailey said it would have seriously hurt Brandon if it connected), prompting one-on-one coaching from Cody on real-but-safe punches; and he was called out by Jimmy for trying to give Jack a super kick with zero super-kick training — he missed the safe zone entirely and caught Jack under the ribs. The most coaching-intensive trainee in the program. June 2 showed the familiar pattern: he struggled with back rolls — more often doing back bumps instead of rolls — but after one-on-one coaching from Jimmy he landed an acceptable roll, and he did well on the bumps in the bump-and-feed drill with a note to feed up a little faster. May 26 showed real signs of it clicking. He was the very first up on snap bumps and bumped too early — the session's teaching moment for 'shadow bumping' — but cleaned it up on attempt 2, and Cade told him he did well on round 2. The recurring theme resurfaced on shoulder tackles: Jimmy called him out for not selling and stressed building the habit so no-selling never becomes a show problem; he sold the very next rep much better. From there he was solid — did well on back elbows, and turned in the strongest heat-drill performance of anyone, drawing positive feedback all around with no notes. Absorbs correction fast and is starting to string together clean, well-sold work. May 19 stayed true to form. On the suplex he went over but didn't get enough jump, so the landing was awkward — Cade Powers had him drill the jump in suplex position a few reps until he could take it cleanly. On flip bumps Jimmy flagged him for dropping his shoulder too much; after coaching he improved, Jimmy demonstrated a couple as reference, and his later reps looked good. Receives a lot of correction but absorbs it within the session. May 15 fit that pattern almost end to end — corrected on hand placement during his lockup with Tyler, multiple corrections on face-bump form during drop toe holds with Michael, flagged for crooked running (Michael and Brent demonstrated correct form before a better third attempt), then drilled turnbuckle running for refinement after coaching on how to sell being thrown into the corner. On the trading drop-toe-hold drill he got leg-placement pointers as the giver with Nick, much better on the second attempt, and ran a couple of clean reps giving to Kirkland after. **The bright spot of his session: when Jimmy came out to spot-check face bumps, Dalton got an explicit positive flag for good execution** — first piece of unprompted praise from Jimmy in his record. No notes on hip tosses after Brent's walk-through. Returned after missing May 8 and May 12.

trainees.name: Jack
trainees.notes: Age 14. Non-bumping restriction lifted May 5, 2026 — parent always present at training.
trainees.current_summary: July 7 (selling night, back to fuller participation): asked what he does best, he said chops. He sold his bump well and took his line well, with a suggestion to slap the mat when he bumps for more noise. On spot one (paired with Hailey) the full-speed run went well after a walkthrough — told to jump a little more into the hip toss, and Hailey helped refine his form. His standout was the promo round: his first promo was strong enough that Jimmy and Kytel had him cut a second on the fly with a different gimmick, and it was just as strong. Continues on a fast track. June 30: still on light work only after his recent hospital visit. He worked a shortened chain-wrestling session with Brent — told to loosen up a bit and given form recommendations plus hold-transition tips from Jimmy, otherwise no flags — and sat out the practice matches on medical grounds. June 23: recently discharged from the hospital, so he trained only in a very limited capacity and was absent from most of the session's notes — no technical record this night. June 16 stayed on the fast track. Lockup with Brandon was clean by the second rep (both bumped on the first). His standout was a major first: he took his first-ever DDT — got pre-coaching since he'd never done it, turned in a good first attempt with just a slight head bump, then later took one from Kaleb cleanly with no head contact and gave Kaleb a good-looking one in the trading round. On strikes, Cade had Jack hitting him while the trainers refined his hand placement and form, and he was very good by the end. Drop-kick rotation came together on his second attempt (needs more jump height), and he worked his enzuigiri attempts up to a good rep before also trying the step-up version successfully. The brief-coaching-then-clean-execution pattern holds. June 12 added two more firsts that both landed, plus a small safety note on the receiving end. Clean front rolls and good superman rolls. First time GIVING headlock takeovers: Cody coached him through it, his first attempt was on the wrong side, but the form was otherwise good and improved from there — no notes by the end. He also took a kitchen sink (a brand-new move for the gym) and did great on his first try, then asked to go again and was clean on the second rep too — the textbook brief-coaching-then-clean-execution pattern holds. On the safety side he was on the receiving end again: Dalton landed on his head during a headlock-takeover rep. Continues on a fast track. June 9: solid striking work — on big boots he was asked to make more contact and looked much better after a form correction, and on punches he drew no notes beyond pulling his hand back a touch less. The night's safety note involved him on the receiving end: Dalton, with no super-kick training, attempted a super kick on him, missed the safe zone, and caught him pretty good under the ribs — Jimmy called Dalton out for it. Ten-plus weeks in and still on a fast track. June 2 added a new first: he'd never done back rolls before, got a coaching pass from Jimmy, then executed two great back rolls — the pattern of brief coaching followed by clean execution holds. Across the bump-on-contact drill he did well but was noted twice for bumping a bit early. Cut a short but good promo with Dalton. May 26 was a tidy bumping session: a timing note from Cade on his first snap bump, then he did well across the shoulder-tackle round with a coaching point on not getting crooked, and did well on back elbows with notes on selling. His standout was the heat drill — remarkable selling with no notes or negatives at all, including the choke/eye-poke selling pointers the trainers covered. Continues the pattern of brief coaching followed by strong execution. May 19 produced the session's standout moment: Jack didn't feel comfortable executing the suplex at first and opted to wait, so the crash pad was brought out to soften the bump and remove the psychological block — he then took it flawlessly, asked to go again (second clean rep), had the pad removed, and took the suplex fine without it. He rode that confidence the rest of the night. Also first to volunteer for the body slam (clean take), and on flip bumps had a good first rep (could get a touch more height) with much-improved reps after a 'straighten up' note. The crash-pad breakthrough is a textbook example of how to coach him through hesitation. May 15 was a heavy first-time session: first time on the headlock-to-wrist-control drill (Brent walked him slowly through it, then he picked it up quickly), first time taking a drop toe hold from Michael (clean after the coaching pass), first time giving a drop toe hold to Nick (clean on the second attempt with refinement reps that all looked good). Got a hesitation note on his first hip toss, much better on the next rep. Kirkland coached him on foot placement in his lockup with Brent — after the note everything looked great — and Brent flagged his lockup with Nick as looking really good. Got a leg-placement coaching point on his initial face bump too. May 12 was first time on lockup-to-headlock transition, first time as the jumper's base on a leapfrog, first time as the hip-toss thrower, first promo. Pattern holds: brief coaching, then clean execution.

trainees.name: Nick
trainees.notes: Jack's dad. Officially joined the program April 14, 2026. Left the program June 2, 2026 after disclosing pre-existing back and nerve problems — decided he didn't want wrestling to exacerbate them. History preserved.
trainees.current_summary: Left the training program on June 2, 2026 after disclosing pre-existing back and nerve problems, concluding he didn't want wrestling to worsen them. His final logged session (May 26) was a safety setback that may have factored in: on his first snap bump he ghost bumped, landed wrong, and hurt his back, sitting out the rest of the night. Eight sessions in. May 26 was a setback on the safety front: on his first snap bump he ghost bumped (bumped too early), landed wrong, and hurt his back — he was asked not to bump for the rest of the session and sat out the shoulder-tackle, back-elbow, and heat-drill rounds. Worth watching that the early-bump habit doesn't recur, since it's now caused an injury. Prior to this he'd been the easiest trainee in the gym to coach. May 19 he took a body slam with a refinement note on staying stiff and not sandbagging, then took another from guest trainer Kytel and executed it flawlessly; as a body-slam giver he needed to mind foot placement but it went well overall. Notably, he held his own in a full five-minute practice match as the babyface against heel Hailey — selling her control segment (leg stomps, the foot slam) and then hitting the comeback with drop-downs, three clotheslines, and a roll-up for the win. Real growth from rep work into match structure. Six sessions in and still the easiest trainee in the gym to coach. May 15 went almost note-free across every block: Brent coached him and Jeff on looking strong in the lockup-to-corner push and both looked good after; clean run through the headlock drill; no notes on the initial face bump; took the drop toe hold from Michael clean after a coaching pass; volunteered to take from Jeff (Jeff's first time giving) and Jeff did well on it; gave a drop toe hold himself with no execution issues; clean as the hip-toss recipient. The May 12 spot-one timing/coordination issues paired with DC were not specifically called out as Nick's. Continues to absorb corrections on the first rep.

trainees.name: Brandon
trainees.notes: Joined the program May 5, 2026 from Cody McCulley's personal roster. Has been training for a while and has worked a few matches at TAW.
trainees.current_summary: June 30: worked chain wrestling paired with Cody with no notes or flags. In his five-minute practice match he beat Michael — absorbing a long heel control segment before making the comeback with chops, forearms, an enzuigiri, and a finishing Vader bomb. TAW experience still showing. June 23: paired with Dalton on dropdowns and flagged for slowness, but when Cody jumped in to run the ropes for him his speed was much better — more an opening-pairing issue than a skill gap. No notes on hip tosses or arm drags. In his five-minute practice match he worked babyface against heel Michael, taking a long control segment (big boot, uppercuts, claw, dropkicks, near-falls) and firing back with splash dodges, a return splash and chops (one redone because the crowd called it weak), and a spear before eating the finish. Jimmy flagged him for needing to watch his legs and leg placement. Nine sessions in; TAW experience still showing. June 16 was another clean night. Lockup with Jack was good by the second rep (both bumped on the first), very good selling, a clean DDT give (paired with Jacob Renzo), and drop kicks done without the crash pad — both good, though on the second he only kicked with one foot. His enzuigiri built to a great third attempt. His TAW match experience keeps showing in how cleanly he works. Eight sessions in. June 12 was a clean night. Good front rolls and superman rolls. On headlock takeovers as the giver he got one coaching point on foot placement after his first attempt, then did much better with no further notes. He also took a kitchen sink (new move) and did well, and did well on drop kicks on his first and subsequent attempts. His TAW match experience keeps showing in how quickly he cleans things up. (Joey stepped away and missed his fireman's-carry execution.) Seven sessions in. June 9: clean striking work — no notes on his big boots, and he handled the double down with Dalton fine. He was on the receiving end of Dalton's dangerous closed-fist punch (Hailey noted it would have seriously hurt him had it connected), through no fault of his own. Six sessions in. June 2: good back rolls, paired with new trainee Damian to help teach lockups, and was attempting a new leg-drop move with Michael when the ring broke. His promo with Jimmy started well but fell flat at the end when he ran out of things to say — a stamina/material gap to work on. May 26 was a strong bumping session — on snap bumps his second rep was a touch slow, which he self-corrected by calling it out himself after getting up (good self-awareness), and rounds 2 needed no feedback. On shoulder tackles he did well and Cade had him stick for a beat after the bump for selling; on back elbows he got a form note. Capped it with a very strong heat drill, doing well throughout. His TAW match experience continues to show in how cleanly he absorbs new material. May 15 was almost entirely clean — Kirkland coached him on making the lockup struggle look realistic in either direction (one of his first lockups), and from there everything ran clean: clean drop-toe-hold form and execution taking from Michael, clean face bump in Jimmy's spot-check, clean as the drop-toe-hold giver with Jeff. No notes on the headlock drill from Brent. May 12 was his first time on spot one — Michael walked him through the leapfrog and up-and-over and Jimmy commended him directly. The earlier May 12 form flags (tailbone landing on bumps, drifting on the rope-crossing drill) did not repeat on May 15. Joining from Cody's roster with TAW match experience tracks with how quickly he absorbs new spots.

trainees.name: Jeff
trainees.notes: Joined the program May 5, 2026 from Cody McCulley's personal roster. Relatively new to training.
trainees.current_summary: July 7 (selling night) was a clean, low-key outing. He sold his bump well and took his line well. On spot one he was paired with Dalton — the corrections landed almost entirely on Dalton, with Jeff sharing only the flag for not remembering who takes the shoulder tackle. In the four-corner drill Cade worked him and his selling was good with no flags, and he cut a solid promo. June 30 added a match win. On chain wrestling with Tyler he was reminded about headlock head placement, with Jimmy coaching the pair on body placement and transition work, and both did great. In his five-minute practice match against Brent he got the win — the match featured a contested early bell (Jeff scored a pin but Brent didn't kick out in time on an early finish) that the commissioner deferred to the crowd, who voted to continue; Jeff closed it out later. June 23 added a notable first. No notes on his hip toss. On arm drags — his first time ever TAKING one — Cody gave him pre-coaching and his first attempt was good, just needing a little more jump; Kytel then gave him a second that also went well, other than his head hitting the mat a bit. In his five-minute practice match against Damian he worked through lockups, strike exchanges, a hip toss, a suplex, and chop trades, finishing with a bear hug into a submission for the win — both wrestlers were flagged for strike work. Eleven sessions in. June 16 was a solid night with one mishap. He did quite well on the lockup with Kaleb (both attempts good) and was doing well on the selling drill until he hit the ropes wrong, which cut his rep short. He gave a good DDT (paired with Michael). The one coaching flag was on Irish whips — Cade noted he stutter-steps into the turnbuckle and had him redo it as one fluid motion. Drop kicks were good (needs a touch more foot height), better on the second. Ten sessions in. June 12 was a strong outing on the new material. Clean front rolls and good superman rolls. He was the last to give headlock takeovers and every execution was good — no notes, a clear high point given how correction-heavy that drill was for others. His one growth edge was drop kicks: like Tyler he struggled to get enough jump height, though his final attempt got up (albeit a bit low). (Joey stepped away and missed his fireman's-carry execution.) Nine sessions in. June 9 added two firsts that both came together. His first-ever big boot was a little light on the first couple attempts (making sure he could get his foot up to the safe-zone height), then he did great. On punches his first was a little slow and light, but after switching to a different style it looked great. Also handled the double down with Dalton fine. Eight sessions in. June 2 was a clean, low-key night: did well on his back rolls and did well on the bump-and-feed drill with no flags. May 26 showed clear forward motion on the bumping fundamentals that have been his growth edge. On snap bumps he did well, with the useful coaching point that a snap bump being fast doesn't mean everything else has to speed up. On shoulder tackles he did well but got notes to float less / cover less distance, and to bump less crooked and sell better — then on the back-elbow round Cade told him he took the best bump Cade has seen him take, a real milestone. Solid heat drill to close. Crookedness and selling are still the things to keep tightening, but the trajectory is up. May 19 was a developmental session focused on his bumping fundamentals. On the drop-down drill he started getting up a touch early on his first rep, took light correction, then was much better. On the suplex, Cody worked the form and sequence with him first, but he didn't have enough jump height — the clear takeaway was that Jeff should refine flip bumps before re-attempting the suplex. On flip bumps he initially used his hands to support himself upside down; he worked onto the crash pad and got useful cues (throw the arms down for momentum, come in tall before initiating the flip rather than leaning into it), with form noted as improving by the end. Bumping mechanics are his current growth edge. May 15 was almost note-free in the early going: Brent coached him and Nick on looking strong in the lockup-to-corner push, both looked good after; clean run through the headlock drill (no notes); good first drop-toe-hold attempt with just a small forearm-placement note; gave drop toe holds to Nick (Nick volunteered to take from him) and did well. The one real flag of the session was on hip tosses — he flipped early on his first attempt, then subsequent attempts came together after posting and placement coaching. May 12: clean run through spot one with Hailey (his first time on the sequence) and first-ever promo paired with Brandon, with Jimmy stepping in to demonstrate. Picks up corrections fast on the next rep.

trainees.name: Damian
trainees.notes: New trainee, joined June 2, 2026. Name was previously spelled 'Damien' in earlier notes; corrected to 'Damian' June 16, 2026.
trainees.current_summary: July 7 (sixth session, selling night): asked what he does best, he said bumps — and Jimmy used his first bump to teach that you sell even in training; Damian's next bump earned a commendation for selling, and he took his line and sold it well too. On spot one he ran it with Michael for the first time (half speed to learn, then full speed) and turned in a good, very-well-executed full-speed run with great selling. In the four-corner drill his selling was good but Kytel called him a little robotic (Cade demonstrated); it was much better when Dalton worked him, and he then worked Dalton through the corners with no notes. Capped it with a strong promo timed almost exactly to the minute. A strong all-around night. June 30 (fifth session): worked chain wrestling with guest trainer Jaxson, who coached him on form, placement, and transition work. He won his five-minute practice match over Kytel with a schoolboy pin, but was flagged for not selling enough — kicking out too strong without selling the offense. June 23 (fourth session): no notes on hip tosses or arm drags. In his five-minute practice match against Jeff he worked through lockups, elbow and forearm exchanges, corner spots, a suplex, and chop trades before being put away with a bear-hug submission. He drew two flags: both wrestlers were flagged for strike work, and Kytel flagged him specifically for stomping the heel when he was supposed to be working face — an early lesson in staying in character. June 16 (third session): Cade coached him through the lockup-to-headlock transition and it looked good. Strong selling, with Jimmy giving him advice on how to work and sell against a bigger opponent. His strikes really improved after form coaching. Good DDTs both given (paired with Dalton) and taken. Solid drop kicks after asking Kaleb for pre-coaching — just needs to get both feet up in the air — and his enzuigiri was close, needing a bit more leg height. June 9 (second session): on double downs Hailey flagged the timing as off; Jimmy got down with him to coach the get-up timing and after a ref-counted second attempt it looked much better (don't move too much, heel moves first). On big boots he had no notes giving on the first attempt and took it fine; his second attempt his toe was a bit too pointed — good execution overall. Did well on punches with form refinement from the trainers and senior trainees. First session June 2, 2026. Opened on back rolls and did well for a first-timer; some form issues on his second round (expected this early), corrected quickly after light coaching from Jimmy and looked much better. Paired with Brandon to learn lockups. Part of Jimmy's wrestling-psychology discussion, who asked the new trainees about their goals and what brought them to wrestling.

trainees.name: Jacob Renzo
trainees.notes: New trainee, joined June 2, 2026. Distinct from former trainee Jacob Justice (inactive). Removed from the program June 30, 2026 for booking himself in a match at a promotion without express consent from Cody or Jimmy, despite only having two training sessions under his belt.
trainees.current_summary: Removed from the program June 30, 2026 after booking himself in a match at an outside promotion without permission from Cody or Jimmy — with only two training sessions in. Jimmy used his removal as the opening teaching point of that session, and Hailey cited the Mass Transit vs. New Jack ECW incident as a cautionary example. June 16 (third session): clean on the lockup → headlock → shoulder tackle drill (pointers on keeping the opponent's head up so they can sell to the crowd), great selling with no notes, and clean strikes with no criticism. On enzuigiri, Jimmy noticed something off and had him go again; Kaleb spotted that he was planting his foot, and after the correction his next reps were good — just needs his leg a bit higher. June 9 (second session): on double downs (paired with Damian) Hailey flagged the timing as off; after Jimmy's coaching and a ref-counted second attempt the timing looked much better. On big boots he was asked to make a bit more contact, then both he and Hailey did great with the exercise. Did well on punches with some form refinement. First session June 2, 2026. Did well on his first back rolls. Paired with Hailey to learn lockups, and cut a good promo opposite guest trainer Kytel. Part of Jimmy's wrestling-psychology discussion, who asked the new trainees about their goals and what brought them to wrestling.

trainees.name: Jaden
trainees.notes: New trainee, joined June 16, 2026. Was previously in the program in 2025 before stepping away from wrestling entirely in October 2025; because of the long gap she is counted as a brand-new trainee in the system, with this prior experience noted here.
trainees.current_summary: First session June 16, 2026. Did well on the lockup → headlock → shoulder tackle drill paired with Hailey. On the selling drill she got quick feedback that she needs to make more sound while selling. Her strikes needed improvement — feedback centered on form and being more animated. Took her DDT a little funky but good, and on the trading round her given DDT looked good and her take was no longer funky. On drop kicks she struggled to get enough height or rotation, so Jimmy had her drill just jumping and turning without the kick motion, then added the kick back in and she executed it; with someone taking it from her, her last couple attempts were both good. Enzuigiri form was good apart from not enough height. Returning to the program after a gap (prior experience in 2025), counted as a brand-new trainee.

trainees.name: Jbird
trainees.notes: New trainee, first session June 30, 2026. 'Jbird' is the only name on record so far — full/real name TBD.
trainees.current_summary: First session June 30, 2026. Worked chain wrestling paired with guest trainer Kytel, and was coached through it on pacing, foot placement, following Kytel's lead, and general form and placement. Brent also worked with him one-on-one for a bit. Called a good first attempt overall. Did not take part in the practice matches. A promising debut with the expected first-timer coaching load.
